data.title: Voyager Technologies: Sell The 139% Post-IPO Rally In This Space Company
data.description:
Voyager Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:VOYG), a commercial space and defense company, soared 139% in its IPO debut, raising $383 million. In this report, I discuss the company’s activities and valuation, explaining why I believe the stock is a Sell at this point.

Voyager Technologies: Focusing On Space Growth With Inorganic Growth Objectives

Voyager Technologies, formerly known as Voyager Space, was established in 2019 and is headquartered in Denver, Colorado. The company operates through three segments:

  • Defense and National Security
  • Space Solutions
  • Starlab Space Stations.

The company has grown through a combination of organic growth and inorganic growth, as the company acquired seven companies for vertical as well as horizontal integration. The Defense and National Security segment accounted for 51% of the sales in 2024 and 66% of the Q1 2025 sales.

The segment includes Defense systems with a $78 million contract supporting Lockheed Martin (LMT) on the Next-Generation Interceptor program with market opportunities for missile defense interceptors, kill vehicles, hypersonic missiles, and re-entry vehicles with defense technologies potentially being repurposed for civil space use. The Signal Intelligence Systems segment includes the company’s analytics platform with signal processing, geolocation technologies, and AI-driven analytics. Communication Technologies includes laser and radio frequency solutions while the company designs, develops, and manufactures technologies for spacecraft control and navigation in its guidance, navigation, and control systems segment. The company also has an AI-powered Edge Computing segment for computing in space integrated with Palantir’s (PLTR) operating system.

Space Solutions accounted for 49% of sales in 2024 and 34% of Q1 2025 sales and includes advanced space technology for solid propulsion technologies, while the space infrastructure includes the Bishop Airlock, which is the first and only permanent privately owned part of the ISS. The Space Science Mission Management provides missions including overseeing missions for customers and satellite deployment.

The Starlab Space Solutions segment is the most probably existing one. The joint venture with Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), MDA Space (MDA:CA), and Palantir aims to provide a replacement for the ISS for microgravity science and research in space. The ISS is set to be de-orbited in 2030, which could put the company at the forefront of facilitating research in space. At the same time, we do note that Starlab is partially funded through NASA’s replacement program and the current budget cuts at NASA could alter funding and replacement requirements.

In 2024, NASA accounted for 26% of sales, followed by 17% for Lockheed Martin, 8% for Airbus and 8% for the U.S. Air Force. The remaining 41% consisted of other customers, of which four parties accounted for 4% or more of the sales individually.

Voyager Technologies Had A Strong IPO

A report from Seeking Alpha detailed that the company initially looked to issue 11 million shares valued at $26 to $29. This would indicate $286 million to $319 million in gross proceeds. The company ended up issuing 12.3 million shares priced at $31 per share, raising $383 million. Net proceeds from the IPO are expected to be $348.9 million and $402.3 million if options to purchase additional shares are exercised.

The stock price surged to $73.95 and is currently trading at around $60.70. So, the stock price is substantially higher than the IPO price but has retreated from its highs, and we should be mindful about a post-IPO dip.

Assessment Of Voyager Valuation

If we look at the revenues, we do see that revenues increased 5% in 2024 and 14.1% in the most recent quarter. We do, however, also note that operational losses have been widening. In 2024, the loss widened from $14.2 million to $48.4 million, and in Q1 2025 we saw losses widening from $11.9 million to $26.3 million. Adjusted EBITDA showed a $30 million loss in 2024 compared to a $1.4 million profit a year earlier, and the quarterly results showed a $21.4 million loss compared to a $14.8 million loss a year earlier.

In 2024, operating cash burn was $25.5 million, up around $10 million year-on-year and there was a $27.8 million cash use for investing activities compared to a positive cash flow from investing activities of $11.6 million a year earlier. For the three months that ended in March 2025, the operating cash burn widened from almost $7.9 million to $14.4 million, while the investing cash burn increased from $5.4 million to $9 million.

Free cash flow burn widened from $5.7 million to $53.3 million in 2024, driven by higher Starlab CapEx, and we also see this back in Q1 2025 on top of the increasing operating cash burn. The company has a pro forma cash position of $465.2 million after the raise, and that is relatively comfortable. However, we do note that the current cash burn rate provides a cash runway of five years. That is definitely not bad, but if operating cash burns wide or does not taper while capital expenditures continue to grow substantially, there is a risk for that runway to become substantially smaller.

The company has a $58 million term loan, which it intends to repay from its $200 million revolving credit facility, maturing in 2029. The company also has $10.5 million in convertible notes and $25 million in convertible notes, bringing the total long-term debt to $90.5 million. If the company pays all maturities from its cash pile, the runway shortens to less than four years.

Voyager Technologies Looks Like A Risky Bet

Running a price-to-sales valuation assuming 15% growth in revenues, in line with the Q1 growth rate and against a stable share count in combination with a price-to-sales ratio for the aerospace and defense industries and Rocket Lab (RKLB), we get an array of price targets.

I consider Rocket Lab to be the gold standard for emerging space companies. So, using the median of that company seems fair, and that would point to a price target of $50.92 this year and $58.56 next year for Voyager Technologies. The stock is currently trading at $52.38 and given the possibility of a post-IPO price decline and the possible need for capital increases in the future at this point, I would take profits and attempt to buy at lower prices. I also ran an EV/Sales assessment which showed a $20.20 price target based on typical values for aerospace and defense companies and a $50.18 price target based on the median EV/Sales using TTM sales. For 2025, the price target would be $52.63 and $57.19 in the year after.

So, the investment case is not necessarily unattractive, but it is also not the most attractive one given continued cash burns and the initial post-IPO surge.

Conclusion: Sell Voyager And Consider Buying Back Later

I believe the safest course of action right now given the post-IPO surge in the stock price is actually selling and taking profits and buying back at lower prices that offer more compelling upsides. The investment case is not necessarily unattractive, but we do note that the company’s losses and cash burns have widened. So, the IPO was needed to accommodate achieving scale. In other words, it is likely going to get worse before it gets better, as is the case with many emerging space companies, and if that is also reflected in the stock price, the downward pressure on VOYG stock will be stronger than upward pressure in the near term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Recession Watch: My Favorite Recession Indicator, Mid-June Update
data.description:
When does a recession start? In the US, the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER), which calls the official recessions in the US, has always defined recessions as broad economic downturns that include downturns in the labor market, such as declines in employment and significantly rising unemployment, neither of which have happened in this cycle.

My favorite recession indicator is the weekly data on the people who filed for unemployment insurance at least a week earlier and are still claiming unemployment insurance because they still haven’t found a job, what the Labor Department calls “Insured Unemployment.”

The black slanted line is the Recession Marker. The levels that entail a recession have risen over the decades as total employment has risen. This growth of employment over the decades causes the Recession Marker line to be slanted upward.

The current level of Insured Unemployment is still historically low, and far below the Recession Marker for this year of about 2.7 million.

The prior three business-cycle recessions – not counting the Pandemic which was a lockdown, not a business cycle recession – came after Insured Unemployment had surged to:

  • 64 million in December 2008, beg. of Great Recession
  • 56 million in March 2001, beg. of 2001 Recession
  • 49 million in July 1990, beg. of 1990 Recession.

Today, the four-week moving average rose to 1.914 million, as the weekly total rose to 1.956 million, according to the Labor Department.

This shows that newly laid-off people – their numbers have been fairly low historically – are having a harder time finding a new job, than they did some months ago.

So it’s not that there is a big wave of job cutting, there isn’t, and employment overall continues to grow. But employers have slowed absorbing the people that have been laid off, and the number of people on Unemployment Insurance has been increasing at an accelerated pace and in early May started exceeding the mid-November level. The purple columns indicate recessions.

If this trend doesn’t reverse… While the level itself remains relatively low, and quite a bit below the recession marker (black line), the trend is going in the wrong direction at a now significant pace.

This trend has occurred before, and then it reversed before reaching anything near critical mass, and the economy kept plugging along just fine.

But if this trend persists, if it does not reverse, but just keeps heading higher toward critical mass, the indicator is going to ring a recession warning bell.

Not a “lagging indicator.” These weekly data for unemployment insurance benefits are the earliest indicators of systemic job losses. The data tracks the number of people receiving unemployment benefits after their initial claim.

A business-cycle recession starts in a specific month, as called out by the NBER. My favorite Recession Indicator gave advance notice of the beginning of the past business-cycle recessions, without having given false positives (indicating a recession when there was none).

The NBER identifies the start-date of a recession, often months after the fact. So the NBER’s determination is lagging and backward-looking. But the start-date of the recession, as called out by the NBER, was indicated well ahead of time by this Recession Indicator.

It’s weekly data, released only a few days after the end of the reporting week, and is the most current. It’s not based on surveys. And it has not given false positives yet, unlike some others, including the yield curve and the Sahm Rule which registered recessions in a strong economy.

I use the four-week moving average (which the Labor Department also publishes) because the week-to-week data jump up and down as big states, when they miss the weekly deadline to process all their unemployment claims data and then submit it in the following week, cause the weekly figure to be low in one week and then high in the next. All states do that from time to time, but the big states move the needle. This kind of stuff has nothing to do with the labor market or unemployment and needs to be filtered out by averaging out the weekly data over four weeks.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AMD Stock: Why It's Going Up And Should Continue Going Up
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) shares have rallied in the last couple months as trade war tensions have eased, AI has continued to drive demand for the company's GPU offerings, and EPYC has maintained its dominance in the server CPU market. Despite fears over market share saturation and slowing growth, I think AMD stock offers a high-upside alternative to the likes of Nvidia (NVDA) as the AI market continues to expand and as AMD consolidates and leverages its lead over a struggling Intel (INTC).

In my last piece of AMD, I discussed the company's Q4 results and the stock's subsequent drop as investors were apparently underwhelmed; in contrast, I saw strong AI revenue growth, encouraging new product launches, and a dominance in consumer offerings that AMD had not seen in its entire history. I recommended a Strong Buy and the stock has since risen about 9% relative to the S&P 500's -1%. That article can be read here.

Of course, there have certainly been some bumps between then and now, namely the Trump administration's trade war with China and other countries, which cratered stock valuations in late March and early April. However, since the easing of those tensions, the market has mostly recovered and fundamentals are coming back into focus. For AMD, that means investors are once again looking at growth, addressable market, and competition.

Let's do a quick recap of the company's Q1 results:

As we have come to expect in recent quarters, revenue growth has continued to impress with 36% YoY overall as datacenter and desktop/gaming revenues soared. But perhaps most impressively, revenue only declined 3% QoQ relative to Q4 despite Q1 typically being the weakest quarter of the year for AMD and other tech manufacturers. And as further evidence of the impact AI demand and market leadership are having on AMD's operating leverage, gross margin expanded 200 bps YoY to 54% in the quarter, operating margin expanded 300 bps to 24%, and EPS grew 55%.

Much of this is driven by the aforementioned rising demand in the datacenter as hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Google (GOOG), and Meta (META) rush to out-spend and out-build each other in a race to what they hope is a lucrative future built on AI workloads for businesses and consumers alike. This trend has massively benefited AMD as its EPYC server CPUs and Instinct GPU line have secured design wins across enterprise customers large and small, leading to $3.7 billion in revenue in Q1, up 57% YoY.

Demand for Genoa and Turin, EPYC's 4th and 5th-gen offerings respectively, has also been strong and has allowed AMD to make further inroads into Intel's server CPU market share as the latter's struggles have continued to snowball. AMD's GPU expertise has also allowed it to bundle EPYC CPUs with the company's MI300 and MI350 series GPU accelerators, which have proven to be an attractive value proposition to those companies that have decided to eschew the exorbitant pricing of Nvidia's Hopper and Blackwell GPUs (more on this discussion later).

While AI usually takes center stage in discussion about AMD's prospects, the company's consumer segment has become an unsung hero as its desktop CPU offerings have stormed into the lead and have demonstrated strong growth. Revenue for the Client segment rose to $2.3 billion in the quarter, up 68% YoY, mainly on the strength of Zen 5-based CPUs. The Client And Gaming segment, which combines desktop CPUs and semi-custom products (i.e. mostly gaming consoles), saw operating profit jump to close to $500 million (+109% YoY) in Q1 even as semi-custom revenues declined 30%, indicating that AMD's consumer-facing CPUs are beginning to turn into a real cash cow. After years upon years of selling low-end CPUs at razor-thin margins and surviving only at Intel's mercy, this successful turnaround is nothing short of miraculous.

There were some fears early on that Zen 5 would underperform due to less-than-expected performance gains over Zen 4, but the affordability and effectiveness of, specifically, the company's 3D V-Cache boosted CPUs like the Ryzen 7800X3D, which sports higher L3 cache to improve gaming performance, proved to be extremely popular with consumers. Intel's continued floundering certainly didn't hurt either.

AMD also provided the following guidance for Q2 2025:

At the midpoint, revenue of $7.4 billion would represent 26.7% YoY growth and gross margin of 54% would be up about 100 bps, both of which are bullish signs for investors. There is the matter of export controls potentially eating into margins and the bottom line, but as of Tuesday, the U.S. and China have reportedly finalized a trade agreement that could lift these restrictions and avoid an inventory charge. Regardless, I think the Q1 results and Q2 guidance re-affirm the growth narrative for AMD and should give confidence to shareholders going into 2H2025.

In the datacenter, AMD is nearing the release and ramp of its MI355X AI accelerator, which as I discussed in a previous article, will provide a more substantial leap over its predecessor the MI325X relative to the marginal upgrade that the MI325X was over the MI300X. The MI355X has been fine-tuned for the company's target market: inferencing workloads.

AMD pulled the release date up from 2H2025 to mid-year 2025 to better compete with Nvidia's Blackwell family of GPUs, and built its feature set around this niche. The processor will support the company's new CDNA 4 architecture, which adds support for FP6 and FP4 precision, sports a ~1.8x compute performance increase over the MI325X, operates with 8 TB/sec bandwidth (about the same as a Blackwell B200), and has a whopping 288 GB of HBM3E memory relative to 192 GB for a B200. This GPU was designed to provide an alternative to Nvidia in inference workloads and I think, based on the specs and customer interest, that AMD has succeeded, which will sustain datacenter growth into the end of the year.

The company already has plans for the MI400 series in 2026, which will likely be manufactured on TSMC's (TSM) N3P and go head-to-head with Nvidia's Rubin architecture. AMD is betting that its acquisition of ZT Systems will allow it to implement scalable AI solutions for both training and inference workloads for this next generation of processors. Nvidia still has a moat with its CUDA software stack, but AMD is undoubtedly closing the gap.

In the server CPU space, AMD has continued to build on its previous momentum, capturing 27.2% of unit share (+3.6% YoY) and a whopping 39.4% of revenue share (+6.5%) in Q1. The most interesting thing about these numbers, as was the case with the Q4 2024 numbers, is just how significantly the revenue share gains are outpacing the unit share gains, which is further evidence that EPYC is the premium offering in the space -- AMD's margins are soaring, while Intel's continue to languish.

However, there might be competition on the way. While this may sound like a broken record for those who have been tuned in for Intel vs. AMD for the last few years, Intel is claiming that its new line of processors will shift the balance of power back in its favor. Recently, Intel's issues with yields on its latest manufacturing nodes have prevented these claims from becoming reality. Will this time be different?

The answer to that question all comes down to Intel's ability to scale its upcoming, cutting-edge 18A manufacturing node. This node, if it comes to fruition, will put Intel back in the game against TSMC and therefore back in the game against AMD. Just recently, the company confirmed that it was aiming for a 2026 release for Diamond Rapids, a server CPU lineup that will likely be based on 18A and compete with AMD's 6th gen EPYC Venice CPUs, which will likely utilize TSMC's N2 node.

Personally, I am fairly bullish on 18A and Intel's prospects in general in the server CPU space, but I don't think that undercuts the potential for AMD to continue reaping benefits from this market. At this point I think there are two likely scenarios: 1) Intel and AMD will stabilize in the next few years into a lucrative x86 server duopoly to the benefit of both companies or 2) Intel continues to struggle as 18A underwhelms and AMD dominates and gains even more market share.

I see AMD as a winner in both of these scenarios, which is why I'm not too concerned in either case.

I don't have much more to add on the desktop CPU side of things mostly because that area tends to be derivative of whatever is happening upstream on the server side, but I think the progress AMD has made in mindshare with its latest offerings will prove to be sticky going forward even if Intel does manage to narrow the performance gap with 18A and the next generation of processors.

Investor Takeaway

AMD stock has underperformed over the last year as investors have tempered their expectations about the company's ability to snag a piece of the AI pie from Nvidia and others. While early AMD AI accelerator offerings have received lukewarm reception, the company is rapidly advancing and improving its architecture and has made significant strides towards becoming a player of note in the inferencing space. AMD's EPYC server CPUs are now best-in-class and have been siphoning away market share from Intel at an impressive pace, while Zen 5 has propelled the desktop CPU segment into a significant bottom line contributor.

While there are some risks here, I think AMD is well positioned to reap the benefits of its target markets long-term. I remember a few years back when EPYC was first starting to pay dividends, the company's GPU business was struggling mightily. Its cards had worse performance AND higher power draw than Nvidia's comparable offerings, and there were rumblings that perhaps AMD would be better suited spinning off GPUs and sticking with CPUs. At the time, I argued that this would be a massive mistake, primarily because I believed AMD having the expertise to design both CPUs and GPUs gave it a competitive advantage over Intel and Nvidia.

The landscape has certainly changed since that time and I admittedly did not predict the advent of AI or the GPU arms race it would set off, but I still think that this is where AMD's value lies: this two-pronged approach offers diversification, confers performance, logistical, packaging, and cost synergies, provides exposure to multiple lucrative markets, allows the design of unique solutions like APUs (which have secured billions in gaming console revenue), and more.

So for this reason and the other catalysts I've mentioned throughout this article, I am re-iterating AMD as a Strong Buy.

Thanks for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PDO: Strong Income Value
data.description:
Fixed income-focused closed-end funds such as the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:PDO) make attractive value propositions now that inflation is trending down and is approaching the Federal Reserve’s long-term inflation target of 2%. With prices rising more slowly now, the Federal Reserve has a strong incentive to lower federal fund rates which could be a catalyst for the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund in 2025, especially for the CEF's fixed income investments, such as high-yielding credit and mortgage securities. The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund also outperformed the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), a fixed income-focused CEF with a longer return history, in the last year.

Previous rating

I rated the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund a strong buy in my last work on the CEF in September 2024 -- A Rate Cut Winner With An 11% Yield -- mainly because I liked the fund’s asset allocation and focus on mortgage-backed securities. Similar to mortgage REITs such as Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY), fixed income-focused closed-end funds are likely to return to a period of net asset value growth if the Federal Reserve lowers interest rates. While the Federal Reserve has so far avoided additional federal fund rate cuts, I believe the Fed will enact more rate cuts in the second half of the year, which could trigger a re-pricing of the fund's fixed income assets and lead to a higher premium to net asset value.

Strong yield-focused investment proposition for dividend investors

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund is a fixed income-focused CEF whose portfolio is dominated by mortgage securities. In total, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund has $1.7B in assets under management, which makes the closed-end fund significantly smaller than the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund which has $6.5B under management.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund has made non-agency mortgage investments the leading investment category in the fund… which is a major change for the CEF since my last coverage in September 2024. Non-agency mortgage investments now represent 23.7% of all investments compared to 25.0% previously. What also gained in importance (weighting) in the fund’s portfolio is emerging market debt, indicating that the fund’s management sees more yield opportunities outside of the U.S. Emerging market debt currently represents 10.1% of fund assets compared to just 6.3% when I last covered the ETF last year.

High-yielding credit, which previously dominated the asset mix, is now represented in the portfolio as the second-largest investment category… with a share of 21.8% (as opposed to 27.4% in my last coverage). I like that the fund is looking for more yield opportunities outside of the U.S. which could make the closed-end fund more attractive to investors as its diversification profile is enhanced. As of today, about 71% of the fund's assets are invested in the U.S. market.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund is still, despite its changing in portfolio weightings, very much a rate-sensitive fixed income play. If federal fund rates get lowered in the second half of the year, which I expect, fixed income instruments in general (mortgage investments, mortgage-backed securities, bonds etc.) are set for potentially significant valuation tailwinds as these assets tend to appreciate during falling-rate periods. The inverse relationship between price and interest rates is, therefore, what makes the closed-ended fund attractive as a long-term income vehicle for investors, especially in a market that is seeing a material drop in inflation.

PDO’s valuation

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund is valued based on net asset value, which essentially reflects the current market values of all of PDO's individual securities. The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund has not had the best net asset value record in recent years because the CEF has been weighed down massively by the Fed’s aggressive rate increases in the 2022 and 2023 periods.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund as well as the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund, therefore, experienced significant valuation pressure in terms of its net asset values. In the last three years, both CEFs have lost about one-fifth of their value, in terms of NAV.

Currently, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund is trading at a 3.61% premium to net asset value, which as of Wednesday, was $12.99 per share. The premium, however, is still very much below the 1-year average which may indicate to dividend investors that the closed-end fund is now undervalued: the average net asset value premium for PDO was 4.6% in the last three years which reflects a discount of 22% compared to the historical valuation.

The premium for the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is currently 12.4% and while it is higher than the premium for the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund, I still like the CEF for its high long-term capital returns: A Golden Buying Opportunity.

Considering that both CEFs have lost about the same amount (in percentage terms) of their net asset value in the last three years, and the portfolio structures are very similar, I believe the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund now makes a superior value proposition for income investors looking not only for a high yield, but also for capital appreciation potential in a falling-rate world.

Risks with PDO

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund is set for NAV tailwinds, but only if the Federal Reserve follows through with actual federal fund rate cuts later this year. If the Federal Reserve were to maintain high interest rates, despite falling inflation, the expected re-pricing catalyst related to the closed-end fund's fixed income assets may not be realized. What would change my mind about PDO is if the Federal Reserve failed to lower federal fund rates in the second half of the year or if the CEF were to become too concentrated in a single asset category (more than 30% of its fund net assets).

Final thoughts

I still very much like the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund, and its bigger cousin, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund, as both CEFs have a favorable asset mix and focus on rate-sensitive mortgage assets… that can be expected to revalue higher in a higher-rate world. In my opinion, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund is widely underrated (relative to PDI) given that inflation is falling and that the CEF offers investors an 11.4% dividend yield. Investor interest in the PDO as a long-term investment vehicle may significantly increase, however, once the market gets a clearer sense of the Federal Reserve’s rate policy and sees the fund's net asset value grow.

data.title: VB: This Is Why Patient Investors Should Remain Confident With Small Caps
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:VB), which gives investors exposure to small caps in a simplified way. The thesis of buying small caps was very clear at the beginning of the year, due to expectations of tax cuts, but those have yet to materialize.

In this article, I will develop arguments that show why these assets have not yet performed well and why investors should be patient and wait for key events that should lead to an increase in small caps, and consequently in the VB ETF.

Vanguard Small-Cap ETF

Before we delve into the thesis, let's take a closer look at the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF. This ETF tracks the CRSP US Small Cap index, which includes American small caps.

For comparison purposes, another peer to this ETF is the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSEARCA:IWM), which tracks the Russell 2000. Let's compare the two over the past 3 years.

The Vanguard Small-Cap ETF has $147 billion in assets under management and a management fee of just 0.05%. The VB ETF has over 1,300 holdings, and the largest has a stake of just 0.45%, making it a highly diversified ETF. Below are the top holdings.

Expectations With Small Caps

The stock market was surrounded by expectations in 2025 with the inauguration of President Trump, especially small cap investors. The opportunity was very clear: the new administration would reduce taxes, and this would benefit the profits of these companies.

However, as we approach the midpoint of the year, the prices of American small caps have not yet risen, on the contrary, the VB ETF has declined 2% year-to-date, as we can see below.

In this article, my intention is to provide three reasons for patient investors to remain optimistic about the small cap thesis.

First Argument

The big trigger for American small caps is the tax cuts, which will increase the profits of these companies. It appears that investors remained skeptical about this until May, however, a big step was taken recently with the approval of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in the American Congress. As we can see below, the small caps should benefit the most.

Investors appear to be waiting for possible changes in the Senate. Approval in the Senate and presidential sanction could be a big trigger for the rise in the shares of these companies.

Second Argument

The chart below shows how small caps are extremely sensitive to interest rates. The fact is that inflation in the US is under control, and although it is not known when rates will be cut again, the financial market projects more rate cuts in 2025.

Another benefit for small caps when interest rates fall is that these companies tend to have short-term floating-rate debt, so the reduction in debt service should also boost the net income and the stock prices of these companies.

Third Argument

Finally, the impacts of the trade war seem to me to be underestimated by some investors in the context of small caps. Large companies, especially the magnificent seven, have extremely robust supply chains, unlike small caps.

An escalation of the trade war will make it difficult for small caps to reshape their supply chains, in addition to reducing their bargaining power with suppliers. However, President Trump announced that the trade deal with China is done, which may reduce tariff risks for small caps. All these arguments corroborate a positive view of the case.

Valuation

Many of the small caps in the ETF may not be profitable, so we will analyze the valuation using the historical EV/EBITDA multiple. EBITDA is considered a proxy for cash generation and should provide us with more assertive information.

Over the past 5 years, the average of the companies present in the ETF traded at 21x EBITDA at their highest and at their lowest at 10x EBITDA, for an average of 15.5x EBITDA. The current multiple is 13.5x EBITDA, an upside of 14.8% that corroborates the recommendation to buy the VB ETF.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Each of the arguments listed has risks. The tax cut bill is quite bold and has even sparked discussions between President Trump and Elon Musk. If it is approved as it stands, investors may become more pessimistic about the US fiscal scenario, and small caps may have a negative impact on their prices due to the increased risk.

As for interest rates, the FED has shown a firm stance to start cutting, despite criticism from President Trump, and the European Central Bank has been cutting rates for some time. Small cap prices may be negatively impacted if the FED takes much longer to cut interest rates.

As for the trade war, although President Trump has stated that the China trade deal is done, he has stated that final approval is required. Therefore, we cannot say that the tariff risk has been eliminated. Finally, SA's powerful Quant tools recommend that investors hold the ETF, which differs from my recommendation to buy.

The Bottom Line

The small cap buy case seemed very clear at the beginning of the year, and many investors believed it. However, the catalysts for the thesis are slower than expected, but that does not mean they have disappeared.

Tax cuts are on the table and should have a big impact on small cap results. Additionally, although there is little visibility on when interest rates will be cut, the consensus is that they will be cut, which is another big trigger. Finally, the reduction of commercial risk is another tailwind for small caps, and it seems to be coming.

Based on this analysis, I recommend buying the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF. In favor of all the arguments above, we have a robust upside of 14.8%, which gives the case a good risk-return ratio.

data.title: JPC's Leverage Strategy Could Supercharge Your Returns (Or Sink Them)
data.description:
The Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:JPC) is a taxable fixed income closed-end fund [CEF] designed to provide investors with high income and total return by investing in preferred and other income-producing securities. JPC currently has a forward distribution of $0.80/share, resulting in a yield of roughly 10%.

JPC is inherently designed to provide investors with fixed income through the investment of preferred shares and hybrid securities like contingent capital securities, as well as corporate and taxable municipal debt and common equity. JPC takes an 80/20 approach with 80% of the portfolio consisting of preferreds and hybrids and 20% consisting of debt and common equity. At least 50% of the investments have investment grade ratings at the time of purchase with the other proportion being high yield.

For the preferred shares, JPC invests across both institutional and retail preferred shares, primarily denoted by the differentiated part value of $1,000/share and $25/share, respectively. According to the fund’s most recent report, the strategy overweighted institutional preferreds as a result of the relative underperformance of the retail preferreds. As part of this, the fund focused its investment strategy on floating rate, fixed-to-floating rate, and fixed-rate reset shares in order to take advantage of the declining reference rate back in March 2025. In general, a lower reference rate helps an investor determine the appropriate value of the preferred shares similar to bonds with interest rates moving inverse to the price.

Theoretically, when the reference rate is declining, the appeal of fixed income increases, potentially resulting in value appreciation. This can benefit JPC as part of its total return strategy. Using the 10-year treasury futures as a reference, we can visually see the inverse impact on JPC when yields are rising and falling.

Given this factor, JPC may have some inherent risks to consider relating to inflation expectations. Referencing the 5x5, 5-year inflation expectations 5-years ahead, inflation expectations remain above the Federal Reserve’s 2% target, potentially suggesting that the Fed may remain prudent in reducing the Federal Funds Rate, denoted as the Secured Overnight Financing Rate [SOFR], which presently sits at roughly 4.30%.

If the Fed were to reduce the Federal Funds rate, fixed rate preferreds might theoretically appreciate in value as the coupon rate will yield a higher spread over the base rate. In turn, floating rate securities will adjust to the base rate, potentially deteriorating the income component that provides investors value during times of increasing rates. The opposite is true for fixed-rate preferreds during times of increasing rates as more secured assets may be considered more appealing, thus reducing the value of the fixed-rate preferreds.

Given the risk of inflationary pressures following the enactment of unilateral tariffs, there may be the potential risk of the Fed either holding rates steady or increasing rates if inflation were to increase at an extraordinary rate. Nonetheless, some are expecting that the Fed will lower rates in the second half of 2025 despite the economic uncertainty.

Accordingly, the portfolio strategy employs a certain amount of leverage in order to amplify income for CEF holders. 15% of the total investment exposure is created with the use of debt borrowings, 12% in reverse repo, and 10% in variable remarketed rate preferred, creating the 37% leverage exposure. Accordingly, the annualized leverage cost sits at 5.39%, essentially acting as a benchmark for yield in order to earn a spread.

Overall, JPC is a broadly diversified portfolio strategy with roughly 270 individual holdings with the top 10 holdings spread across banks and insurance institutions.

JPC is a relatively expensive fund to hold, charging investors an expense ratio of 1.71%.

Considering peer preferred income funds, JPC is relatively aligned with most funds net of iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), which charges a 46bps fee.

Overall, JPC has outperformed PFF by a wide margin over all periods, potentially making the fund an appealing investment.

From a risk factor, JPC typically faces larger drawdowns during periods of stress despite outperforming PFF. Depending on the investor’s risk tolerance and duration, JPC may be a more appealing option if seeking longer-duration income.

Risks Related to JPC

Investing in non-fixed rate securities can be interest rate-sensitive, appreciating in value during times of rate decline and declining in value when rates increase. Given the general expectation of the Fed reducing the key rate in 2025 in the midst of economic uncertainty, floating-rate securities may face some pressure.

The fund may also face certain risks during an economic downturn. Given the fund’s exposure to high yield fixed income securities, the risk of default exists to a certain degree. Given the diversification of the portfolio strategy, the risk related to default is relatively low.

JPC holds a certain amount of leverage in order to target a higher return for investors. Assuming the rate on the securities purchased with leverage outweighs the cost of leverage, the CEF will benefit from the spread. Conversely, the fund may suffer losses if the cost of leverage outweighs the return on the security, which may be the result of a declining floating rate component of the underlying asset.

With the risk of the Fed reducing interest rates, JPC may face certain exposure risks to its floating-rate preferreds.

Given the uncertain economic outlook, I am assigning a hold rating to JPC.

data.title: NZF: Buy High-Yield Muni And Expect Good Return In The Next 12 Months
data.description:
Introduction

The S&P 500 has embarked on a remarkable V-shaped recovery and is just inches away from the all-time high. Many investors may plan to look for alternatives to boost the total return for 2025. I have recently added muni funds to my portfolio, mostly with Nuveen Municipal Credit Income Fund(NYSE:NZF). I believe that the fund, with about an 8% yield (tax-exempted) and a strong possibility of a few bps gain in narrowing the NAV discount, stands to offer better opportunities for double-digit total return in the next 12 months. I will initiate a buy rating on NZF and look to add more shares if the NAV discount goes deeper.

NZF Overview

NZF is one of the large Muni funds managed by Nuveen, focusing on the income that can be exempt from federal tax. It pays a monthly distribution with a yield of 8.04% at the moment. This appears to be a very attractive high yield, not to mention the benefits of the tax exemption.

The portfolio hosts about 700 muni bond holdings with an effective maturity of 20 years, according to the Nuveen official site

The following shows the breakdown of the top sector and holdings. Notice that the top sector is the Health Care accounting for 23.1%, and the transportation sector, which is traditionally a stable one for muni bonds, is weighted at 21.6% at the number two spot.

The healthcare sector may be a relatively weak sector due to the uncertainty of the current administration's policy. The Utilities sector (8.7%) is also pretty resilient.

The monthly distributions have been stable for the past 12 months despite the yield having climbed up to slightly above 8%. There are RoCs in the distribution at about one-third in the recent payments, as shown below.

NZF AUM is about $2.39 billion, which is one of the largest funds in muni bonds. The fund NAV has performed relatively well with a -3.81% discount, which is one of the smallest in its space. The leverage is very high at 41.60%, but slightly better than the other bigger Nuveen muni funds, such as the one I covered in the past. Note that NZF charges an expense ratio of 1.60%, which is higher than some of the other Nuveen funds.

An interesting contender for double-digit total performance gain

The muni fund has been out of favor for a few reasons in recent months, some of which are headwinds such as inflation worries due to tariffs and the 10-year treasury's yield being relatively high above 4.4%. The recent Moody's cut for the US credit rating and the rapid bounce recovery from the equity market in the past few weeks have put the bond funds in check.

On the other hand, Investors may start to feel a bit uneasy when the stock market could make a new high soon, with both the S&P 500 index and the Nasdaq 100 index very close to the record high. It may be questionable how much more the equity market could rise from here until the year-end.

On a relative basis, the muni bonds seem to have a setup where the bad news has been priced in the bond market. It may signal a stage bottom and provide entry points for the interested investors. One direct result of the lower price is the higher yield. The yield for NZF has been over 8% as indicated earlier, which is a high yield attractive to income seekers.

On top of that, the muni bond tax exemption is very likely to stay based on the current proposal of the bill "One Big Beautiful Bill Act". So the "real" distribution could be higher for the people who need to pay a higher income tax.

Compared to the stock market, I would argue that an 8%+ gain from the current level of the S&P 500 index could be more difficult to accomplish than NZF's yield for the next 12 months, unless the US economy sees a much-improved growth outlook. In addition, there is a NAV discount (-3.8%) that could be narrowed by a couple of base points. The total return could reach a double-digit gain in a relatively straightforward way.

Should the interest rate go down to neutral?

One of the obvious catalysts for muni CEF to move up in price is the FED interest rate cut. It is a known objective for the FED to bring the interest rate down to the normal "neutral" level. Although projected in the later part of 2025, it is with high certainty that the FED will cut once to set the rate towards around 3.5%-4%. According to Forbes, "a July or September cut is now more likely", assuming the inflation stays put. So as we approach closer to July (or September for that matter), the muni bond fund will be more likely to respond positively by itself in a natural progressive way.

There is a concern about the tariff causing inflation to move higher. I believe that the tariff impact on inflation could be rather moderate, as the most recent inflation data looks lower than expected, and the overall tariff rates could be more on the lower end after the negotiations. On the positive side, the muni bond market is the local (state) debt for the local projects without a direct connection to the tariff affair itself.

I have been adding NZF shares, taking advantage of the recent weakness in the fund price. This is not only for the portfolio allocation adjustment for the income portion, but also with a better total return in mind for the next 12 months or so. If there are some interest rate cut "surprises", such as an earlier cut or more cuts, I would expect these to become potential catalysts, which could drive the fund price higher in 2025.

Risk Analysis

The rate cut will not be possible if the US inflation worsens in the future. The bond funds will be hit hard by the high inflation. Muni investors should keep a close eye on the inflation indicators.

The high leverage on NZF (over 40%) will make the fund perform much worse if the rate is higher, because of a higher cost for borrowing. The highly leveraged funds are rate-sensitive, risky assets.

The other key risk is the US debt, which could go out of control if not managed in time. On one hand, the Moody's downgrading of US credit to below AAA is not new and is expected to have little impact in the short run, considering such downgrading has been over 3 years, Fitch Ratings and S&P in 2023 and 2011, respectively. If the US debt is indeed going to be bloated continuously, the local muni market could be severely impacted at some point.

Closing Thoughts

NZF is a high-quality muni fund that is rated 4 stars by Morningstar.com. The muni bond market seems to see the worst in the short term. With the yield rate over 8%, I expect the NFZ to be an attractive holding that could offer a double-digit return in the next 12 months or so. I rate NZF a buy and recommend it to investors who are actively looking for opportunities in the fixed-income market.

data.title: ZipRecruiter: The Slide Continues
data.description:
My first look at ZipRecruiter, Inc. (NYSE:ZIP) was just over two years ago in May of 2023. The stock was selling at around $17.00 a share back then and I concluded that initial article around this company with a Sell rating. Since then, the equity has ground down to less than six bucks a share. Has the equity fallen enough to be in the "buy zone"? An updated analysis follows below.

ZipRecruiter, Inc. is headquartered in California in the city of Santa Monica. The company's business model is quite simple. It operates a platform where job seekers are able to apply to jobs with a single click and employers are enables employers to post jobs and access other features. The stock currently trades near $5.75 a share and sports an approximate $525 million market capitalization. The company continues to add capabilities to its platform. This includes Zip Intro that was implemented in 2024 that allows employers and job seekers together for face-to-face video conversations.

Recent Results:

ZipRecruiter posted its Q1 numbers on May 8th. The company posted a net loss of $12.8 million for the quarter. This was up from a net loss of $6.5 million in the same quarter a year ago. ZipRecruiter did have a positive adjusted EBITDA of $5.9 million. That said, that was down sharply from adjusted EBITDA of $20.8 million in 1Q2024. Lower revenues and higher marketing and personnel expenses were the primary drivers of lower net income and Adjusted EBITDA.

Revenues fell 10% on a year-over-year basis to $110.1 million, some $1 million above the consensus analyst estimate and down one percent sequentially from the fourth quarter of 2024. Revenues were slightly above the midpoint of management's previous guidance. Revenue per paid employer was up 2% from the same period a year ago but down 10% sequentially from the fourth quarter. It should be noted that the first quarter is historically the weakest quarter for ZipRecruiter.

Leadership also provided this cautionary note around hiring:

We started the year with cautious optimism. However, we now see employers adopting a ‘wait-and-see’ attitude, reflecting widespread uncertainty in the near-term U.S. economy."

Furthermore, management said they have seen no increased hiring activity in the first five weeks of the second quarter when first quarter results were posted. Management did reiterate its belief it will start achieving year-over-year revenue growth by the fourth quarter of this year.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Despite the fall in the stock price, it is hard to find the stock Wall Street is more negative on at the moment. Since first quarter results hit the wires, seven analyst firms including Evercore ISI, UBS, and Barclays have reissued Hold ratings. Price targets proffered range from $6 to $10 a share, with most in the $6 to $7 range.

Approximately seven percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. Several insiders were consistent, frequent but generally smalling sellers in the shares throughout 2024. That insider behavior has continued in 2025. Interestingly, a beneficial owner disposed of roughly three million shares in May.

ZipRecruiter listed approximately $468 million worth of cash and marketable securities on the 10-K the company filed for Q1 against $543 million in long-term debt. The company incurred just under $7.4 million in net interest expense in the first quarter.

Conclusion:

ZipRecruiter had a net loss of $12.9 million on $474 million revenue in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has ZipRecruiter losing 54 cents a share as sales fall five percent to $449 million in FY2025. They project losses to be reduced to 38 cents a share in FY2026 on revenue growth in the low teens.

Even with the stock falling by two thirds since the last time I looked at ZipRecruiter. The company is projected to continue post losses for the foreseeable future. While ZipRecruiter has the balance sheet to sustain those losses, the equity offers little for investors here.

This is especially true as the jobs market continues to deteriorate. Job openings are down sharply from their pandemic peak and the trend remains down. In addition, the unemployment rate, after hitting a low of 3.4% in April of 2023, has slowing ground higher and now stands at 4.2%, and is likely to go higher.

Until ZipRecruiter, Inc. moves closer to profitability, tariff and trade policies are settled on, and job market dynamics improve... ZIP continues to be a name I would avoid.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Falls
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 4.0 percentage points to 36.7%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 18th time in 19 weeks and is above 30% for only the 10th time this year.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 3.8 percentage points to 29.7%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 47th time in 49 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 7.8 percentage points to 33.6%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 28th time in 30 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 11.8 percentage points to 3.1%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 19th time in 21 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how, if at all, they changed their approach to investing recently.

Here is how they responded:

  • I’ve become much more conservative/cautious: 17.2%
  • I’ve become slightly more conservative: 23.7%
  • I’ve switched around some investments, but only made modest changes overall: 24.9%
  • I’ve become more aggressive: 9.8%
  • No changes: 24.0%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 36.7%, up 4.0 percentage points
  • Neutral: 29.7%, up 3.8 percentage points
  • Bearish: 33.6%, down 7.8 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Nathan's Famous: A Cash Cow In A Hot Dog Suit
data.description:
Another solid quarter in the books for this century-old Coney Island legend. I’d say that’s worth celebrating—with a hot dog in each hand.

My call on Nathan’s Famous (NASDAQ:NATH) is aging like a fine hot dog—over 35% total return in just six months. That's a big win, especially when you compare it to the S&P, which dropped about 1.2% over the same stretch.

It’s been a rough patch for the market overall, thanks to the 'Trump Tariffs' and the entire stagflation narrative that brought the market down after February.

Aside from a few bright spots I've highlighted here in SA, most restaurant chains—especially fast-food players, and even the fast-casual darlings that crushed it in 2024—are in the red so far this year.

But let’s not forget: these days, Nathan’s isn’t just another asset-light restaurant chain. It’s a licensing powerhouse.

This not only gives it a business model resilient to tighter discretionary spending but turns it into something any investor loves: a good old-fashioned cash cow.

But beyond the strong Q4 FY 2025 numbers—and the full-year performance we’ll get into—I want to focus on something that really lit a fire under the stock: the potential buyout from Smithfield Foods (SFD).

Given Nathan’s long-standing partnership with John Morrell Foods (a Smithfield brand), the market didn’t ignore the noise. In fact, the stock popped nearly 15% in a single day—February 13—on the back of those rumors.

To give you an idea, in FY 2025 alone, the Product Licensing segment, tied to Smithfield, generated $7.9 million for Nathan’s, becoming the company’s second-largest source of revenue and the largest chunk of its EBIT (about $7.85 million, dwarfing the loss from restaurant operations and exceeding by almost 5x what they earn from the Branded Product Program).

It actually makes a lot of sense.

Smithfield could tap into the synergies with John Morrell, simplify operations, and still keep the iconic Coney Island spot alive (because some things just can’t be touched).

And if the offer comes with a nice premium (the company has only $48.1 million of long-term debt, a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 1.28x, and much lower if we consider net debt), I doubt shareholders would complain.

Rumors come and go—but sometimes, they turn out to be true. Just look at Irth’s offer for Papa John’s (PZZA), which was confirmed today. Irth already held a 5% stake, making the move feel very real from the start.

Now, what about Nathan’s? Smithfield doesn’t appear to have a meaningful stake, at least not publicly.

But you know who does? Chairman Howard Lorber—he owns nearly 25% of the company, a stake worth about $105 million. And here’s the kicker: he’s pushing 80. If there’s ever been a time he might have handed over the reins and sell to Smithfield, it could be now.

Just like with Papa John’s, whether the M&A actually happens isn’t the main point for me. What really matters is the valuation story. That’s what we have in front of us right now.

Despite this, we can do some exercises to estimate an estimated premium on a possible transaction through comparable deals.

According to my experience with this type of business, the acquisition can be done in the following ways:

  • Conservative multiple (less likely due to synergies and debt level): something around 10x EV/EBITDA;
  • Median multiple (most likely and safest): around 15.7x EV/EBITDA;
  • High multiple: around 18.5x EV/EBITDA.

Assuming an EBITDA of $42 million for FY 2025, we would have a median EV of $659.4 million. Subtracting net debt of $20.3 million, we would find an Equity Value of $639.1 million, or approximately $155 per share.

Note that this would be a premium of approximately 34% to where the stock is trading today at $115. Again, as I said, it would be a good deal for shareholders.

Now that I've cleared that up, let's take a look at the results for Q4 FY 2025.

The Hot Dog Is Still On Fire

Starting with Q4 FY 2025, Nathan's reported revenue of $30.78 million, increasing at least 6.2% year-over-year.

Looking across segments, we have the following outlook:

  1. Branded product program: It was $20.04 million (or 65% of total revenue for the quarter), growing 9.6% year over year. Just to remind you, this revenue includes sales of products to food service chains—not necessarily franchisees. This is definitely a bright spot, especially at a time when food service has been sluggish, with more guests opting to dine at home;
  2. Licensing Royalties: Totaled $7.9 million in the quarter, representing 5.3% year-over-year growth and representing 25% of total revenue. This revenue stream corresponds to royalties from Smithfield sales (through John Morrell);
  3. Restaurant Sales: $2.27 million in the quarter, down approximately 13.4% year over year. This represents 7% of Nathan's total revenue in the quarter. If you read my QSR articles, you'll know that they are losing their reputation and value wars due to the phenomenon called the 'Fast-Food Exodus'. This is no different for Nathan's, which keeps its century-old restaurants only for their symbolic value (since company-owned restaurants usually lose money).

The rest comes from advertising fund revenue, which doesn’t really count as operating income for Nathan’s.

Another interesting angle is to break down the margins by segment.

Just take a look at this:

At the end of the quarter, Nathan's reported EBIT of $6.4 million, down 5.8% year over year.

Note that there is a catch: although food service revenue did not decline, margins themselves fell almost 50 bps year over year. This could be attributed to lower spreads, discounts to customers (given the soft environment for restaurants), or even a less profitable customer mix. It is difficult to say since Nathan's does not make earnings calls.

However, licensing royalties remain strong, and this is what impacts EBIT the most since they are included almost without deductions as operating profit.

With long-term debt down nearly $10 million from the same period last year, Nathan's reported net income of $4.2 million—one of the strongest Q4 results in recent memory. EPS came in at $1.04, up 8.3% year-over-year.

As usual, Nathan's reported strong FCF, at approximately $6.8 million for the year. Since their Capex is negligible ($0.1 million), FCF was $6.7 million. After distributing $2 million in dividends and repaying $0.2 million of long-term debt, they had a cash surplus of $4.1 million.

I really can't stress this enough—Nathan's has a rock-solid business model. It pulls in impressive returns with barely any capital outlay, simply by collecting royalties on its licensed products through Smithfield.

This goes way beyond your usual asset-light restaurant model. There are no franchisees shutting down and weighing on the bottom line, and no constant pressure to pour money into remodeling funds.

And honestly? That’s a pretty sweet spot to be in.

+35% Since Call Start, Still a 'Buy'?

As we talked about earlier, Nathan’s still looks cheap if you’re thinking about a potential buyout—that much is clear.

But what if we take the takeover chatter off the table? Are the shares still a bargain?

Keep in mind, that I had a price target in the $100–$105 range, and we’re now sitting almost 10% above that. I think it’s time we take a fresh look.

To warm things up, let’s do some projections for FY 2026.

Starting with revenue, we can project it to be somewhere around $155.6 million (from a growth slowdown of about 100 bps to 5% year-over-year).

Based on this, we could also project an EBIT of $38.9 to $40 million, with an EBIT margin of 25% or better.

Finally, assuming a 20% annual EPS growth, we would have something around $7.05.

Note that considering a projected EV of $501.11 million, we would be looking at a projected EV/EBIT of 12x to 13x. This is below the median of 15x for Consumer Discretionary stocks.

Using this multiple as a parameter and adjusting for Nathan's capital structure, we would be looking at a price target of $130 to $135 depending on reported EBIT. This is an upside of 13% to 17%. Not bad in my opinion.

Looking at the P/E, Nathan's would be trading at a multiple of 16.3x based on my EPS estimates for FY 2026. As Consumer Discretionary companies are trading at 17.1x. Note that at this median P/E, the price target would be somewhere around $120.50. Considering 20% ​​growth, we would have a PEG of 0.85, which is also positive.

That said, Nathan's could give us an upside in the following ways:

  • Takeover Thesis: 34% Upside from where shares trade today;
  • Organic Growth: Upside of approximately 13% to 17% from capital gains. Add that to a dividend yield of 1.73%, and you have a total return of approximately 14.7% to 18.7%.

That said, I'm reiterating my 'Buy' rating on Nathan's Famous and raising my price target to somewhere in the $125–$130 range. For a company with such a stable, low-maintenance business model and solid free cash flow generation, this still looks like a compelling total return story.
